title: Gate B crowding threshold policy
excerpt: Escalate Gate B crowding when ingress queues spill into circulation lanes or screening delays begin to back up onto the plaza.
body: Gate B crowding requires the crowd-flow team to open overflow stanchions, rebalance screening lanes, and issue a gate supervisor advisory before the queue spreads into shared circulation space.
rationale: Explains when crowding becomes an operational response issue and what the first policy-backed control steps are.

title: Ingress queue relief runbook
excerpt: Dispatch the Crowd Flow Lead to Gate B, open the overflow queue path, and rebalance screening lanes before congestion spreads.
body: When Gate B is backed up, the Crowd Flow Lead owns the first response. Open the overflow path, shift one screening lane if adjacent capacity is available, and post a short ingress advisory to gate supervisors.
rationale: Provides the operational sequence for immediate Gate B crowding relief.

title: Playoff ingress overflow at Gate B
excerpt: Previous Gate B spillback was reduced after overflow lanes were opened and staffing shifted toward screening.
body: A prior Gate B ingress backup cleared after the overflow lane was opened, screening lanes were rebalanced, and the crowd-flow lead coordinated guest redirection from the plaza.
rationale: Shows that the same response pattern has resolved Gate B crowding before.

title: Gate B plaza and overflow path
excerpt: Gate B has an overflow queue path that can be opened toward the outer plaza when screening lines begin to spill back.
body: Gate B sits on the entry plaza and supports an overflow stanchion route that keeps circulation clear while crowd-flow staff redirect guests. Supervisors use this path to protect adjacent gate traffic.
rationale: Adds location-specific context for where crowd relief actions should happen.

title: Crowd Flow Lead dispatch rule
excerpt: The Crowd Flow Lead owns Gate B crowding response, queue redirection, and gate supervisor coordination.
body: Use the Crowd Flow Lead as the assigned owner for Gate B crowding. That role opens overflow lanes, directs guest redirection, and confirms that queue pressure is no longer spreading into adjacent circulation routes.
rationale: Clarifies which staff owner should be assigned for Gate B crowding.

title: Accessibility infrastructure outage policy
excerpt: A guest-impacting elevator outage requires immediate facilities response and alternate accessible route coverage.
body: If Elevator 4 is down, dispatch facilities immediately, station accessibility support on the alternate route, and update guest services with the accessible detour until restoration or confirmed outage status is communicated.
rationale: Explains why an elevator outage becomes an accessibility and facilities response issue.

title: Elevator 4 outage runbook
excerpt: Send Facilities to diagnose Elevator 4, assign alternate route coverage, and provide a restoration ETA or outage confirmation.
body: When Elevator 4 is down, the Facilities Engineer confirms the fault, reports an ETA, and coordinates with guest services so the alternate accessible route stays staffed until service returns or an outage is declared for the event window.
rationale: Provides the facilities-led response sequence for the elevator outage.

title: Elevator 4 door sensor fault
excerpt: A prior Elevator 4 outage was mitigated by alternate route staffing while engineering reset the lift.
body: During a previous event, Elevator 4 failed because of a door sensor fault. The outage stayed manageable only after facilities gave a restoration ETA and accessibility staff covered the alternate route for impacted guests.
rationale: Shows that facilities response plus alternate route coverage has already worked at this location.

title: Elevator 4 alternate accessible route
excerpt: The accessible detour from Elevator 4 uses the East Concourse ramp and requires a staffed handoff when the elevator is unavailable.
body: Elevator 4 serves the lower concourse access path. If it is unavailable, direct guests to the East Concourse ramp and place a staff handoff at the detour entrance so wheelchair and mobility-assist guests do not lose route continuity.
rationale: Adds the location-specific accessible route needed during the outage.

title: Facilities Engineer outage ownership rule
excerpt: The Facilities Engineer owns Elevator 4 diagnosis, restoration ETA updates, and facilities response coordination.
body: Assign the Facilities Engineer when an accessibility-impacting elevator outage is reported. That role confirms the fault state, communicates the restoration estimate, and coordinates the facilities response with operations and guest services.
rationale: Clarifies the staff owner for the facilities response path.

title: Guest wheelchair assistance policy
excerpt: A wheelchair access request requires immediate acknowledgement, guest services dispatch, and a confirmed escort to the destination route.
body: When a guest near Section 112 needs wheelchair access, acknowledge the request immediately, dispatch guest services with the correct mobility support, and confirm the escort path through the accessible route to the guest’s destination.
rationale: Explains why Section 112 wheelchair support is an Immediate guest-assistance issue.

title: Section 112 wheelchair dispatch runbook
excerpt: Send Guest Services to Section 112, coordinate the nearest accessible route, and confirm the guest handoff over radio.
body: For a wheelchair support request near Section 112, the Guest Services Supervisor dispatches the nearest mobility-assist team, confirms the accessible route from the guest’s current position, and closes the loop once the escort reaches the destination.
rationale: Provides the guest-services response sequence for wheelchair assistance near Section 112.

title: Wheelchair escort request near Section 112
excerpt: A prior Section 112 mobility request closed after guest services completed the wheelchair handoff and escort.
body: Guest services resolved a previous Section 112 wheelchair request by meeting the guest near the section, confirming the accessible route, and staying on the radio until the guest transfer and seating handoff were complete.
rationale: Shows that quick guest services response and route confirmation has worked for this exact area.

title: Section 112 accessible route notes
excerpt: Section 112 wheelchair support should route through the lower bowl accessible path with a guest-services handoff at the aisle entry.
body: Section 112 sits on the lower bowl route. Guests needing mobility support should be met at the aisle entry, escorted through the accessible path, and handed off only after the seat approach is clear and confirmed.
rationale: Adds the location-specific accessible route details for Section 112 support.

title: Guest Services wheelchair response rule
excerpt: The Guest Services Supervisor owns wheelchair dispatch, accessible route escort coordination, and guest handoff confirmation.
body: Assign the Guest Services Supervisor to Section 112 wheelchair support. That role coordinates the guest services response, confirms the accessible route, and ensures the escort and handoff are acknowledged before the incident is marked in motion.
rationale: Clarifies the assigned owner for the guest services response.

title: Operations Lead post-event reporting rule
excerpt: The Operations Lead records approved actions, timeline updates, unresolved items, and final wording for the post-event report.
body: Post-event reporting begins once actions are approved. The Operations Lead confirms that the timeline reflects approvals, unresolved issues stay visible, and the after-action wording stays consistent with the operational evidence that drove the response.
rationale: Supports the post-event reporting step without introducing scoring or unsupported certainty language.
